ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised chicken hot pot with rice; 'chicken dish' (FOODON_03315671) used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a braised chicken dish; rice component not separately coded.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for braised chicken hot pot with rice; A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is the primary protein anchor. The rice component and composite dish nature are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to braised chicken hot pot with rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of braised chicken hot pot with rice is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of braised chicken with sauce and vegetables in a small hot pot vessel plus one bowl of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Braised Chicken Hot Pot 黄焖鸡小火锅 (包含一份米饭)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record for this dish (cr_id G00010491). All per-100g values are as reported by HPB with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for braised chicken thigh with sauce (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB does not report saturated fat for this record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed rice-based dishes; white rice GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised chicken hot pot with rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database). The braised chicken and sauce components contribute minimal additional carbohydrate and are expected to slightly moderate the overall GI via protein and fat co-ingestion. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (54.1 g from 450 g serving × 23.95 g/100g) / 100 = 65 × 54.1 / 100 ≈ 35.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 375 mg/100g with insufficient potassium (171 mg/100g) and fibre (2.0 g/100g) offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce, as sodium at 375 mg/100g with a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,688 mg sodium — nearly 75% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — and the low potassium (171 mg/100g, below the 200 mg/100g offset threshold) and low fibre (2.0 g/100g, below 3 g/100g) provide insufficient compensation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-GI carbohydrate load (23.95 g/100g, GI ~65) partially moderated by protein (9.24 g/100g) and fibre (2.0 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should be aware that the included rice portion drives a glycemic load of approximately 35 per serving (high GL), and should consider requesting a smaller rice portion, substituting brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce postprandial glucose excursion; the low sugar content (3.33 g/100g) and moderate protein are partially mitigating factors.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (375 mg/100g), moderate fat (13.54 g/100g) including estimated saturated fat (~3.5 g/100g), and cholesterol (29 mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to the high sodium content (375 mg/100g) which contributes to blood pressure elevation, and the moderate total fat and saturated fat content; requesting reduced sauce, smaller portions, and pairing with fibre-rich vegetables can help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory ingredients (ginger, garlic, mushrooms) offset by nightshade content (dried chili) and high sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of ginger, garlic, and mushrooms in this dish, but should monitor tolerance to dried chili (a nightshade) as solanine may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals, and should request reduced soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Chicken Hot Pot with Rice (黄焖鸡小火锅，含米饭) is a popular Chinese comfort dish widely served in Singapore's food courts and casual dining establishments. The dish consists of bone-in or boneless chicken pieces slow-braised in a richly seasoned sauce made from soy sauce, oyster sauce, dried chili, ginger, garlic, and mushrooms, served in a small individual hot pot vessel and accompanied by a bowl of steamed white rice. The braising process produces tender, deeply flavoured chicken with a glossy, slightly spicy and savoury sauce. The mushrooms absorb the braising liquid and contribute an umami depth, while ginger and garlic provide aromatic warmth. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 255 kcal, 9.24 g protein, 13.54 g fat, and 23.95 g carbohydrate, with the carbohydrate load primarily from the included rice. Sodium is elevated at 375 mg/100g, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) driven by the white rice component, with a high glycemic load per full serving (~35), relevant for type 2 diabetes management. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for most dietary patterns but is not suitable for those avoiding gluten (soy sauce) or shellfish derivatives (oyster sauce). It is a staple lunch and dinner option in Singaporean Chinese cuisine.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id G00010491) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and key secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent.
notes: Fields set to -1 and basis for unavailability: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database record G00010491; the HPB database does not disaggregate total fat into fatty acid sub-fractions for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1 because micronutrient vitamin data are not individually reported in the HPB record G00010491 for this composite dish; no approved secondary source provides these values for this specific preparation. phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 because these trace mineral values are not reported in the HPB record G00010491 and are not available in an approved source for this exact composite dish. water_g is set to -1 because moisture content is not reported in the HPB record G00010491 for this composite dish. All -1 values were confirmed unavailable by consulting the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (foodo.hpb.gov.sg) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat estimated at ~3.5 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for braised chicken thigh with sauce (closest approved-source analogue); this is an adaptation and not directly reported by HPB. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Halal status is unconfirmed as oyster sauce and soy sauce brands vary by vendor; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the specific food outlet. The dish name in the input record includes '(包含一份米饭)' confirming rice is included in the nutritional values.
